$meta.generated: by scripts/lgr_build.py from the canonical dataset in src/data/lgr/
$meta.authoritiesNote: Net revenue budget as adopted, per each council's own budget papers; bases differ slightly between councils (net revenue expenditure vs budget requirement) — see basis per entry.
$meta.bandDNote: Council's own Band D element only (excl. police £292.40, fire £94.73, parish precepts). District residents also pay the LCC county precept.
$meta.metricsNote: Population and place indicators: ONS Census 2021 and official statistics via the ukdemographics data platform (extracted 2026-07).
$meta.licence: Open data — figures traceable to the cited public sources; verify against the primary source before formal use.

$meta.verified: two independent official sources, identical groupings

decision.decidedBy: Secretary of State for Housing, Communities and Local Government

$meta.source: LCC Budget Report 2026/27 (Full Council, 26 Feb 2026) and GOV.UK/MHCLG Revenue Outturn returns, via the AI DOGE Lancashire data pack (aidoge.co.uk)

county.name: Lancashire County Council
county.composition: 84 seats: Reform UK 52, Conservative 9, Independent 7, Labour 5, Liberal Democrats 5, Green 4, Our West Lancashire 2 (LCC political composition, 8 July 2026)

contractsBeyondVesting.$note: Analysis of the AI DOGE contracts register (Contracts Finder/Find a Tender award notices, buyer = Lancashire County Council). Caveats: county contracts only (district contracts not included); 197 notices carry no end date so the straddling total is a floor; Contracts Finder data is supplier/buyer self-reported.

topStraddling.supplier: Wirral Community Health and Care NHS Fou

county.debtNote: External borrowing 31 Mar 2025 (31 Mar 2026 provisional: £953.3m), MHCLG Borrowing & Investment live table. Reserves figure of £345.0m verified exactly against the MHCLG RS return (£359.2m including public health).

unitaries.name: West Lancashire
unitaries.note: "West Lancashire" is the government's own working label for the Blackpool, Fylde and Wyre unitary in its 16 July decision. Final names are not yet agreed, and this is not the current West Lancashire district, which joins South Lancashire

owns.basis: Net expenditure before other government grants

$meta.note: Completed English LGRs 2019-2023, from official sources (MHCLG decision documents, council business cases, KPMG/CIPFA closure and assurance reviews, Hansard, audited accounts). Confidence per figure as flagged; 'not published' means no consolidated official figure exists.

cases.name: Dorset & BCP

transition.note: No consolidated actual published; BCP later needed a £76m capitalisation request and a DLUHC assurance review

savings.realised: Dorset reports £96m convergence+transformation; BCP needed ~£41m invested for £35m net

cases.notable: Divergent fortunes: rural Dorset stabilised, urban BCP hit financial distress

cases.name: Buckinghamshire

transition.note: Under budget; £2.7m underspend rolled into transformation

savings.realised: £75.4m over first 4 years, ~4x the business case

cases.notable: The benchmark success case, delivered despite vesting nine days after Covid lockdown

cases.name: West & North Northamptonshire

transition.note: Later joint estimate £43.5m; no consolidated actual, £953m county balance-sheet split disputed for ~4 years

savings.realised: WNC ~£34m and NNC £17m+ transformation savings reported over three years

cases.notable: Intervention-driven (county s114); the disaggregation dispute is the direct warning for splitting a county

cases.name: Cumbria (Cumberland + W&F)

transition.note: Implementation within budget, but efficiencies deferred; W&F needed ~£26m Exceptional Financial Support in year one

savings.realised: Not realised in the LGR phase (KPMG closure report); disaggregation costs swamped early savings

cases.notable: The 'split a county' precedent: on-budget build, year-one financial support for both new councils

cases.name: North Yorkshire

transition.note: Self-funded from county reserves; ~£6m spent by mid-2023, no consolidated outturn published

savings.realised: £3.1m in year one; savings back-loaded, ~£30m year-one shortfall

cases.notable: Completed pay/job evaluation BEFORE vesting, the only case to avoid legacy terms-and-conditions drift

cases.name: Somerset

transition.note: Districts incurred extra costs on top; no consolidated actual

savings.realised: Only ~£7m by early 2024, too slow to offset care pressure

cases.notable: Financial emergency declared 8 months after vesting; £77.9m capitalisation support incl. £40m to downsize; still on legacy T&Cs ~2 years on

perResidentEnvelope.basis: Budgeted transition cost per resident across the six precedents (Somerset £29 to N. Yorks £62)

impliedTransition_m.basis: Precedent per-resident envelope applied to 1.53m residents; before disaggregation risk, which hit hardest in the county-split cases

lancashire.keyRisk: 15→4 is the most complex disaggregation attempted in this era, closest in kind to Cumbria and Northamptonshire, the two cases where splitting the county drove overruns and multi-year disputes

birmingham.liability: £650m–£760m announced June 2023, accruing £5–14m/month, on top of ~£1.1bn already paid; s114 notice 5 Sep 2023

cumbriaClaim.fact: Aug 2023 (five months after vesting): Cumberland and Westmorland & Furness both received an equal pay claim covering 400+ former Cumbria CC employees (CIPFA external assurance review)

somerset.fact: Workforce still on several legacy terms-and-conditions sets ~2 years after vesting (CIPFA); ~£3m pay & grading programme, ~1,600 role profiles inherited, equal-pay risk cited as rising with delay

northYorkshire.fact: ~3,000 staff TUPE-transferred at vesting; single job-evaluated pay structure completed pre-vesting across all 8 councils

cca.name: Lancashire Combined County Authority (LCCA)
cca.established: Formally launched 5 February 2025 (Lancashire Combined County Authority Regulations 2025; shadow authority first met 27 November 2024)
cca.mayoralNote: Non-mayoral, led by a chair; government has said Lancashire will get a mayor within ~18 months, alongside reorganisation

functions.name: Local Transport Authority role
functions.detail: LCCA becomes the LTA for all three constituent councils from 1 April, with a £640m transport funding settlement over four years

functions.name: Adult Skills Fund
functions.detail: £40.8m adult education/skills budget administered by the LCCA from August 2026 (previously central government)

functions.name: Proposed
functions.detail: Housing, wider education and skills, and environmental functions proposed under the devolution deal

cca.infancyNote: The CCA is in its infancy: strategy and funding are lifting to CCA level, but delivery capacity still sits with LCC and the two existing unitaries. No published disaggregation shows what part of any council's net budget transfers to the CCA.
cca.postLgrNote: After vesting day the CCA's constituent members will be drawn from the successor unitaries; Lancashire's end-state is four unitary councils plus one (likely mayoral) combined authority: five bodies, not four.

$meta.primary: Mercer, Report on the Actuarial Valuation as at 31 March 2025 (signed valuation report)

pensions.fund: Lancashire County Pension Fund (LGPS), administered by Lancashire County Council; day-to-day administration by Local Pensions Partnership
pensions.allFifteenNote: Rates & Adjustments Certificate lists all 15 Lancashire councils as Major Authorities in the single fund, including Blackpool and Blackburn with Darwen, which stayed in the county fund on becoming unitary in 1998

lccContribution.note: £35m whole-council cash reduction following the 2025 valuation surplus; LCC's budget papers assumed ~£21.2m net revenue benefit (reconciliation of the two is inference, not verified)

pensions.nextValuation: 2028-03-31, the day before vesting day; projected surplus ~£3,370m (~137%)

lgrPrecedent.cumbria: Fund NOT split at the 2023 county split: Westmorland & Furness became administering authority for the single Cumbria Pension Fund, Cumberland an employer
lgrPrecedent.northamptonshire: Same pattern in 2021: West Northamptonshire took on the single fund as administering authority, North Northamptonshire an employer
lgrPrecedent.implication: Precedent points to LCPF remaining one fund with one successor unitary designated administering authority (a structural-change-order decision), contextual inference, not a published decision for Lancashire

statutoryProcess.basis: Part 1, Local Government and Public Involvement in Health Act 2007; implementation by Structural Changes Order under s.7
statutoryProcess.procedure: Draft affirmative: the SCO must be approved by resolution of both Houses of Parliament before it is made
statutoryProcess.expected: SCO expected later in 2026 (laying date not yet published); shadow elections May 2027; vesting 1 April 2028

populationCriterion.wording: For most areas this will mean creating councils with a population of 500,000 or more, but there may be exceptions to ensure new structures make sense for an area, including for devolution, and decisions will be on a case-by-case basis
populationCriterion.clarification: Government later described 500k as a guiding principle, not a target; all four Lancashire unitaries sit below it under the case-by-case flexibility

funding.proposalDevelopment: £7.6m across the 21 invited areas (£135,000 per area + 20p per resident). WMS HCWS545, 24 Mar 2025
funding.transition: £63m across LGR areas: at least £900,000 per new unitary, plus up to £150,000 per unitary for children's/ASC/public-health leadership continuity, and up to £1m for complex fire transitions
funding.whoPays: Government's stated expectation is that reorganisation costs are met by councils within the area, with openness to flexible use of capital receipts and borrowing; no LGR-specific Exceptional Financial Support scheme has been published

july16Announcement.scope: 14 areas decided on 16 July 2026: 134 existing councils to be replaced by 38 unitaries; Cambridgeshire & Peterborough and West Sussex deferred
july16Announcement.lancashireRationale: Reflects Lancashire's diverse urban, rural and coastal communities, aligned with existing economic areas and transport links, with a clear structure for future devolution (GOV.UK)
july16Announcement.approvedProposal: The four-unitary option was the proposal submitted by six councils: Chorley, Lancaster, Preston, Ribble Valley, South Ribble and West Lancashire

proposerSavingsClaim.note: The bidding councils' own business-case estimate, not an independent or government-verified figure

july16Announcement.lccPosition: Lancashire County Council's leader opposed the decision, citing affordability, delivery and the scale of change

$meta.note: Cost-vs-savings scenario assumptions. Every parameter is traceable: savings run-rate from the proposers' business case (via lancashire.thelead.uk); realisation rates and cost envelope from the precedents evidence base (precedents.json); equal-pay exposure from Reform UK's published Lancashire LGR analysis (own modelled estimate, Mar 2026); government offsets from government.json. Outputs are MODELLED SCENARIOS, not forecasts.

costProfile.note: Transition spend profile: 10% pre-SCO year, 30% shadow year, 40% vesting year, 20% year after

$meta.offsetNote: 4 x >=£900k transition funding + 4 x £150k leadership top-ups + Lancashire's ~£0.4m share of proposal-development funding

costsVsSavings.runRateNote: Proposers' claimed recurring annual benefit from 2032/33 (their business case; not independently verified)

scenarios.label: Proposers' case
scenarios.basis: Savings as claimed (£81.9m/yr by 2032/33, linear ramp from vesting); transition at the centre of the £50-100m precedent envelope

scenarios.label: Precedent-adjusted
scenarios.basis: 60% realisation of the claimed run-rate, reflecting Somerset (~38% of projection in year one), North Yorkshire (back-loaded) and Northamptonshire (partial); Buckinghamshire's over-delivery is the outlier. Transition cost above centre, reflecting the county-split premium

scenarios.label: Downside
scenarios.basis: Cumbria pattern: savings deferred and largely absorbed by disaggregation; transition at the top of the envelope; the £85.5m modelled equal-pay exposure crystallises post-vesting (as an actual claim did in Cumbria, five months after vesting day)

Lancaster.bandDSource: Council budget resolution, 25 Feb 2026 (Band D uplift 2.99%)

Preston.budgetNote: Includes business-rates retention and technical adjustments; council-tax-funded slice is £16.974m

Ribble Valley.bandDSource: Budget report to Council (Band D frozen 2026/27); awaiting primary-source confirmation

Wyre.budgetBasis: Net expenditure before other government grants
Wyre.bandDSource: Revenue Budget & Council Tax 2026/27, Full Council 5 Mar 2026 (Band D £240.05)

Chorley.bandDSource: General precept, frozen; £215.99 incl. special expenses in unparished area (https://chorley.gov.uk/council-tax/finance)

South Ribble.balanceNote: no external borrowing per MHCLG returns

West Lancashire.balanceNote: includes HRA housing debt; HRA reserves £6.7m excluded

Blackpool.balanceNote: heaviest debt of any merging council, rising
